fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a library that can read and write those Apple-style config files (the XML ones you see in iPhoto libraries, cookie exports, that sort of thing). The parsing side should handle all the usual stuff — nested objects, lists, numbers, yes/no flags, timestamps, raw binary blobs, and make sure text with special characters comes out clean. We had a similar entity-decoding requirement in the old login module, so just follow that same pattern. On the write side, it should spit out well-formed XML fragments or a full wrapped document depending on what the caller asks for. Collections need proper indentation (tabs, not spaces, like we always do), and dictionaries should come out in a consistent order so diffs are readable. Binary content should be line-wrapped the same way we handled it in the export pipeline. The whole thing should be callable from a small adapter that reads a JSON command off stdin and writes plain text to stdout — nothing fancy, just enough to drive it from tests. One thing I keep forgetting to mention: empty plists and comment-only documents should not blow up, they should just report back gracefully. Also the date handling needs to be solid — second-level precision, always UTC. Basically I want developers to stop hand-rolling XML walkers every time someone drops a .plist file on us.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in. On the parse report side, boolean values should come through as the literal strings 'true' and 'false', so examples like first_album_master=true and second_album_slideshow_use_titles=false are exactly what we want to see. And when we write them back out, boolean true should emit a self-closing <true/> element and boolean false should emit a self-closing <false/> element.

Also wanted to pin down the date formatting a little more tightly. All date/datetime/timestamp values need to serialize as <date>YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ</date>, always second precision, always with the trailing Z for UTC. If we get a bare date string like '2020-01-02', pad it to '2020-01-02T00:00:00Z'. On the parsing side, date elements should also be reported back in that same ISO-8601 UTC form with second precision.

For parsed data elements in the data_elements report, the adapter should output three lines per key: '{key}_type=data', '{key}_bytes={N}', and '{key}_sha256={hex}', where N is the decoded byte count and hex is the lowercase SHA-256 digest of the decoded bytes. If the input is an empty <data/> element, that still counts cleanly as 0 bytes and the SHA-256 of an empty byte string.

For the album_library_overview report, please emit 'keys=' followed by the top-level dictionary keys joined with the pipe character '|', sorted in lexicographic order, all on one line. The example to follow is 'keys=Application Version|Archive Path|List of Albums|List of Keywords|List of Rolls|Major Version|Master Image List|Minor Version'. Same sorting rule applies any time we serialize a dictionary too: all keys need to be sorted lexicographically by their text value before emission so the output stays deterministic and diff-friendly no matter what order they came in.

And just to make the formatting piece unambiguous, indentation is a single tab character (\t) per nesting level. Array and dict children each sit one additional tab in from their parent opening tag, and the closing tag comes back to the parent’s indentation level.

Last bit is string/key text handling. During parsing, the XML entity decoding should follow the same behavior as before for '&' → '&', and when serializing we need the reverse escaping of '<', '>', '&' in text values and key text.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: parse_report(operation, report, xml, as_readable_stream) / serialize_fragment(operation, value) / serialize_document(operation, value)

trigger_keywords: empty array serialization
empty dict serialization
self-closing element for empty collections
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty array must serialize as the self-closing element <array/> followed by a newline. An empty dictionary must serialize as the self-closing element <dict/> followed by a newline. Neither should emit opening and closing tag pairs with no content between them.

trigger_keywords: binary data line wrapping
base64 line length
how long are base64 lines in data elements
data element formatting
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Binary data elements serialize base64 content wrapped at 68 characters per line. The <data> opening tag appears on its own line, each 68-character base64 chunk on its own line, and </data> on a final line. This matches the output shown in feature4_1 test cases.

trigger_keywords: empty plist behavior
comment-only document
null result when no value present
what happens with an empty XML document
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A plist document that contains only comments, XML declarations, or DOCTYPE declarations and no actual value element must yield a null/None parse result. The adapter must report this as 'type=null' on stdout. An empty string input (optionally passed as a readable stream) must also report 'type=null'.

trigger_keywords: XML entity decoding in strings
ampersand in parsed text
special characters in string values
entity references in parsed output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: String and key text must have XML entities decoded during parsing. For example, '&' in XML source must become '&' in the parsed string value. Conversely, when serializing strings, the characters '<', '>', and '&' must be escaped as '<', '>', and '&' respectively.

trigger_keywords: full document wrapper
XML declaration and doctype
serialize_document vs serialize_fragment
plist root element wrapping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The serialize_document operation must prepend '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
' before the serialized value and append '
</plist>
' after it. serialize_fragment emits only the value XML with no wrapper.

trigger_keywords: symbol_text kind
how is symbol_text serialized
difference between string and symbol_text kinds
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'symbol_text' kind must serialize identically to the 'string' kind — it emits a <string> element containing the text value with XML-sensitive characters escaped. There is no distinct XML element for symbols; they map to <string> in the output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

are there public test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: line-wrapped the same way we handled it in the export pipeline
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Base64 content inside <data> elements must be wrapped at 68 characters per line, as shown in feature4_1_serialize_binary_data.json expected output

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify off the top of my head — you'll want to look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the expected_output fields there. The test cases are the authoritative spec for exact formatting and field naming.

feature1_1_parse_structured_xml_values.j.description: Parse XML property-list documents into observable scalar, array, and object values while preserving nested field values and boolean values.

feature1_3_parse_strings_and_empty_keys..description: Parse string and key text exactly, including XML entity decoding and empty key names inside dictionaries.

input.xml:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
  <dict>
    <key>key</key>
    <dict>
      <key></key>
      <string>1</string>
      <key>subkey</key>
      <string>2</string>
    </dict>
  </dict>
</plist>

feature1_4_parse_empty_and_encoded_sourc.description: Accept readable XML input as well as text input, ignore comments and document declarations, and preserve declared character encoding for parsed text.

input.xml:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<!-- I am a comment! -->
<!--
  I am a multi-line comment!
  hooray!
-->
</plist>

feature1_5_parse_data_elements.json.description: Parse base64 data elements from XML property-list input into byte streams whose content can be read back without alteration.

input.xml:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
  <dict>
    <key>stringio</key>
    <data>dGhpcyBpcyBhIHN0cmluZ2lvIG9iamVjdA==
    </data>
    <key>file</key>
    <data>AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA
    AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA
    AAAAAAAAAAAAAA==
    </data>
    <key>io</key>
    <data>AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA
    AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA
    AAAAAAAAAAAAAA==
    </data>
    <key>nodata</key>
    <data/>
  </dict>
</plist>

cases.expected_output:
type=object
io_type=data
io_bytes=100
io_sha256=cd00e292c5970d3c5e2f0ffa5171e555bc46bfc4faddfb4a418b6840b86e79a3
file_type=data
file_bytes=100
file_sha256=cd00e292c5970d3c5e2f0ffa5171e555bc46bfc4faddfb4a418b6840b86e79a3
stringio_type=data
stringio_bytes=25
stringio_sha256=dd7f7d430b6345ae1785903935e360bd5b2c9851c6ccd222e665d3eea941e966
nodata_type=data
nodata_bytes=0
nodata_sha256=e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

feature2_1_serialize_text_values.json.description: Serialize text-like values as XML string elements and escape XML-sensitive characters in element content.

feature2_2_serialize_numeric_and_boolean.description: Serialize integer, real-number, and boolean values to the corresponding XML property-list scalar elements.

feature2_3_serialize_temporal_values.jso.description: Serialize calendar dates, date-times, and timestamps into UTC-style XML date elements using second precision.

feature3_1_serialize_arrays.json.description: Serialize arrays as XML array elements, preserving element order and using a compact self-closing element for an empty array.

feature3_2_serialize_dictionaries.json.description: Serialize dictionaries as XML dict elements, sorting keys by their textual form and using a compact self-closing element for an empty dictionary.

feature3_3_serialize_nested_collections..description: Serialize nested arrays and dictionaries recursively while preserving the expected XML tree shape and indentation.

feature4_1_serialize_binary_data.json.description: Serialize byte-stream input as base64 XML data elements with line wrapping suitable for property-list output.

feature4_2_serialize_complete_document.j.description: Optionally wrap a serialized value in a complete XML property-list document with XML declaration, document type, root element, and closing root tag.

cases.expected_output:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<array>
	<integer>1</integer>
	<string>b</string>
	<true/>
</array>
</plist>
